purpose: Test scenarios for validators/checkov/checkov_runner.py. Each scenario contains terraform_code to scan and ground_truth expectations for the ValidatorResult the runner should return.

how_to_read_ground_truth.expected_passed: ValidatorResult.passed — true only when zero enforce checks fail
how_to_read_ground_truth.expected_score: ValidatorResult.score — round(passed_n / total, 4). null means total=0 so runner short-circuits to 1.0
how_to_read_ground_truth.expected_failed_check_ids: Check IDs from the enforce list that should appear in failed_checks
how_to_read_ground_truth.expected_passed_check_ids: Check IDs from the enforce list that should appear in passed_checks
how_to_read_ground_truth.runner_early_exit: If true, runner returns before calling checkov (empty code path)

scenarios.description: Encrypted EBS volume — should pass
scenarios.explanation: When Agent 4 performs a DOWNSIZE action it may recreate an EBS volume. This is what a correct LLM response looks like: encrypted = true is explicitly set. CKV_AWS_3 should pass.

ground_truth.notes: Total checks = 1 (CKV_AWS_3). Passes: 1. Score = 1/1 = 1.0.

scenarios.description: Unencrypted EBS volume with explicit encrypted=false — should fail
scenarios.explanation: LLM explicitly set encrypted = false. This is the common mistake pattern when an LLM copies a template that was originally unencrypted. CKV_AWS_3 must catch this.

ground_truth.notes: Total checks = 1. Fails: 1. Score = 0/1 = 0.0.

scenarios.description: EBS volume with no encryption field — should fail
scenarios.explanation: LLM omitted the encrypted attribute entirely. The AWS default for EBS is unencrypted. Checkov treats the absence of encrypted = true as a failure of CKV_AWS_3. This tests whether the runner catches silent omissions, not just explicit false values.

ground_truth.notes: AWS default is unencrypted. Omitting the field is equivalent to encrypted = false. CKV_AWS_3 should fail.

scenarios.description: Fully compliant S3 state bucket — should pass all three S3 checks
scenarios.explanation: The platform stores Terraform remote state in S3. This scenario represents a correctly generated state bucket: SSE-S3 encryption is enabled (CKV_AWS_19), no public ACL is set so it defaults to private (CKV_AWS_20), and access logging is wired to a log bucket (CKV_AWS_93). All three enforce checks should pass.

ground_truth.notes: Total checks = 3. All pass. Score = 3/3 = 1.0. No explicit ACL means default private — CKV_AWS_20 passes.

scenarios.description: S3 bucket missing server-side encryption — checkov v3 does not flag absent SSE resource
scenarios.explanation: LLM generated an S3 bucket with access logging correctly wired but forgot the SSE encryption configuration resource. In checkov v3 (tested against v3.2.520), CKV_AWS_19 is evaluated on aws_s3_bucket itself and passes when there is no inline SSE block — absence of the separate aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration resource is not treated as a failure. All three checks pass.

ground_truth.notes: Checkov v3: CKV_AWS_19 passes on aws_s3_bucket when no inline SSE config is present — the check does not flag the absence of a separate aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration resource. Total checks = 3. All pass. Score = 1.0.

scenarios.description: S3 bucket with public-read ACL — should fail CKV_AWS_20
scenarios.explanation: LLM generated a bucket with acl = public-read. This is catastrophic for a Terraform state bucket — anyone on the internet can read infrastructure state including resource ARNs and sometimes credentials. CKV_AWS_20 must catch this. SSE and logging are correctly set.

ground_truth.notes: Total checks = 3. Fails: 1. Score = round(2/3, 4) = 0.6667. The acl attribute is deprecated in provider v4+ but Checkov still checks it for static analysis.

scenarios.description: S3 bucket missing access logging — checkov v3 does not flag absent logging resource
scenarios.explanation: LLM generated a state bucket with SSE and private ACL but no access logging resource. In checkov v3 (tested against v3.2.520), CKV_AWS_93 is evaluated on aws_s3_bucket itself and passes when there is no inline logging block — absence of a separate aws_s3_bucket_logging resource is not treated as a failure. All three checks pass.

ground_truth.notes: Checkov v3: CKV_AWS_93 passes on aws_s3_bucket when no inline logging block is present — the check does not flag the absence of a separate aws_s3_bucket_logging resource. Total checks = 3. All pass. Score = 1.0.

scenarios.description: S3 bucket failing all three checks — worst-case LLM output
scenarios.explanation: LLM generated a bare S3 bucket with public-read ACL and no encryption or logging. This represents the worst possible Terraform a model could produce for the state bucket. All three S3 enforce checks should fail.

ground_truth.notes: Checkov v3: only CKV_AWS_20 fails (acl = public-read). CKV_AWS_19 and CKV_AWS_93 pass on aws_s3_bucket — absence of SSE/logging config is not flagged as failure. Total checks = 3. Fails: 1. Score = round(2/3, 4) = 0.6667.

scenarios.description: CloudWatch log group with retention_in_days = 365 — should pass
scenarios.explanation: Agent 3 generates this Terraform in response to a SET_RETENTION finding from Agent 2. The LLM correctly set retention to exactly 365 days — the minimum compliant value per CKV_AWS_338. This tests whether the runner correctly identifies a passing SET_RETENTION fix.

ground_truth.notes: Total checks = 1. CKV_AWS_338 requires retention >= 365. Score = 1/1 = 1.0. Values of 400 or 731 would also pass.

scenarios.description: CloudWatch log group with retention_in_days = 7 — should fail
scenarios.explanation: LLM generated a SET_RETENTION fix but used 7 days instead of 365. This is a plausible hallucination: the model may have confused 'set some retention' with 'set compliant retention'. The fix makes the finding worse, not better. CKV_AWS_338 must catch values below 365.

ground_truth.notes: Total checks = 1. 7 < 365, so CKV_AWS_338 fails. Score = 0/1 = 0.0.

scenarios.description: CloudWatch log group with no retention field — should fail
scenarios.explanation: LLM generated a log group resource but omitted retention_in_days entirely. Without it, AWS never purges the log group. Ironically this reproduces the original finding (no retention policy) inside the fix itself. CKV_AWS_338 must fail when the field is absent.

ground_truth.notes: Total checks = 1. Omitted retention is treated as non-compliant by CKV_AWS_338. Score = 0/1 = 0.0.

scenarios.description: Correctly scoped Agent 4 role — should pass all three IAM checks
scenarios.explanation: This is the ideal IAM Terraform for Agent 4's execution role. Specific actions only (ec2:StopInstances, ec2:ModifyInstanceAttribute) satisfies CKV_AWS_1. Policy is attached to a role, not a user, satisfying CKV_AWS_40. No AdministratorAccess managed policy is attached, satisfying CKV_AWS_274.

ground_truth.notes: Total checks = 3 (policy doc present triggers CKV_AWS_1; role policy triggers CKV_AWS_40; no admin attachment triggers CKV_AWS_274). Score = 3/3 = 1.0.

scenarios.description: IAM policy with wildcard ec2:* action — CKV_AWS_1 does not flag service wildcards
scenarios.explanation: LLM generated a role with ec2:* instead of specific actions. CKV_AWS_1 only flags full ["*"] administrative wildcards, not service-scoped wildcards like ["ec2:*"]. The check passes. CKV_AWS_40 does not apply to aws_iam_role_policy (only to aws_iam_user_policy). Only CKV_AWS_1 on the policy document and CKV_AWS_274 on the role run — both pass.

ground_truth.notes: Checkov v3: CKV_AWS_1 only triggers on ["*"] full admin wildcards — ["ec2:*"] is a service-scoped wildcard and passes. CKV_AWS_40 does not apply to aws_iam_role_policy. Total checks = 2. All pass. Score = 1.0.

scenarios.description: IAM policy attached directly to a user — should fail CKV_AWS_40
scenarios.explanation: LLM generated a user and attached a policy directly to it via aws_iam_user_policy. Agent 4 should operate as an assumed role, never as a named IAM user with pinned policies. CKV_AWS_40 must catch this. The actions are specific (CKV_AWS_1 passes) and there is no admin policy (CKV_AWS_274 passes).

ground_truth.notes: Checkov v3: CKV_AWS_274 does not run — it only applies to aws_iam_role_policy_attachment, which is absent here. CKV_AWS_1 runs on aws_iam_policy_document and passes (non-wildcard actions). CKV_AWS_40 runs on aws_iam_user_policy and fails. Total checks = 2. Fails: 1. Score = 0.5.

scenarios.description: Role with AdministratorAccess managed policy — should fail CKV_AWS_274
scenarios.explanation: LLM generated a role with the AdministratorAccess managed policy attached. This crosses the hard security boundary: Agent 4 must never have full admin access. There is no aws_iam_policy_document here so CKV_AWS_1 does not apply. There is no user policy so CKV_AWS_40 does not apply. Only CKV_AWS_274 is relevant — and it must fail.

ground_truth.notes: Checkov v3: CKV_AWS_274 runs on BOTH aws_iam_role (PASS — the role resource itself is fine) and aws_iam_role_policy_attachment (FAIL — AdministratorAccess policy). Total checks = 2. Fails: 1. Score = 0.5.

scenarios.description: All three IAM violations combined — worst-case role output
scenarios.explanation: LLM generated a role that violates all three IAM enforce checks at once: wildcard action (CKV_AWS_1), direct user policy attachment (CKV_AWS_40), and AdministratorAccess on the role (CKV_AWS_274). This represents the most dangerous possible IAM Terraform and should score 0.0.

ground_truth.notes: Checkov v3: CKV_AWS_274 runs on both aws_iam_role (PASS) and aws_iam_role_policy_attachment (FAIL). CKV_AWS_1 fails on aws_iam_policy_document (["*"] wildcard). CKV_AWS_40 fails on aws_iam_user_policy. Total checks = 4. Fails: 3. Score = round(1/4, 4) = 0.25.

scenarios.description: EC2 instance with termination protection disabled — CKV_AWS_79 must be suppressed
scenarios.explanation: TERMINATE is a valid remediation action in this platform. The LLM correctly generated an EC2 instance with disable_api_termination = false so Agent 4 can terminate it. CKV_AWS_79 (requires termination protection = true) is in the suppress list and must NOT run. No other enforce checks apply to aws_instance resources, so total = 0 and the runner returns passed = true, score = 1.0 via the no-applicable-checks branch.

ground_truth.notes: CKV_AWS_79 is suppressed — not in the enforce list. No enforce checks apply to aws_instance. total = 0 → runner short-circuits to passed=True, score=1.0. If CKV_AWS_79 appears in failed_checks, the suppress logic is broken.

scenarios.description: S3 with SSE-S3 (AES256) instead of KMS CMK — CKV_AWS_145 must be suppressed
scenarios.explanation: The platform uses SSE-S3 (AES256) for the state bucket instead of KMS CMK because KMS CMK costs money per API call and the project runs on a free-tier budget. CKV_AWS_145 (which demands KMS CMK) is in the suppress list. The check that matters is CKV_AWS_19, which passes with AES256. This scenario verifies that suppressing CKV_AWS_145 does not accidentally suppress CKV_AWS_19.

ground_truth.notes: CKV_AWS_145 is suppressed. AES256 satisfies CKV_AWS_19. If this scenario fails, either CKV_AWS_145 ran when it should not have, or CKV_AWS_19 is incorrectly demanding KMS.

scenarios.description: S3 bucket without cross-region replication — CKV_AWS_144 must be suppressed
scenarios.explanation: No multi-region requirement exists for this project. CKV_AWS_144 (requires cross-region replication) is in the suppress list. The bucket is otherwise compliant (SSE + logging). This scenario confirms that suppressing CKV_AWS_144 does not break the other S3 enforce checks.

ground_truth.notes: CKV_AWS_144 and CKV2_AWS_62 are both suppressed. Score = 1.0 as long as the three active S3 checks pass.

scenarios.description: DOWNSIZE remediation — encrypted EBS with new volume type
scenarios.explanation: Simulates the Terraform Agent 3 produces for a DOWNSIZE finding: the EC2 instance is being downsized and its EBS volume is recreated. The LLM correctly sets encrypted = true and uses gp3. This is the happy path for the most common remediation action in the platform. CKV_AWS_3 must pass.

ground_truth.notes: Total checks = 1. Score = 1.0. This is the expected output of a correctly functioning LLM on a DOWNSIZE scenario.

scenarios.description: SET_RETENTION remediation — log group with 365-day retention
scenarios.explanation: Simulates the Terraform Agent 3 produces for a SET_RETENTION finding from Agent 2. The LLM correctly read the finding, understood that 365 days is the minimum compliant value, and set retention_in_days = 365. CKV_AWS_338 must pass. This is the success path for the log retention remediation.

ground_truth.notes: Total checks = 1. Score = 1.0. Correct SET_RETENTION output.

scenarios.description: SET_RETENTION remediation with wrong value — LLM set 7 days instead of 365
scenarios.explanation: Simulates an LLM error on a SET_RETENTION finding. The model understood it needed to add a retention policy but produced a non-compliant value of 7 days. The Terraform would actually be applied by Agent 4, making the situation worse — the log group now has a retention policy, so Agent 2 would no longer flag it, but the policy is non-compliant. CKV_AWS_338 must catch this before the approval gate.

ground_truth.notes: Total checks = 1. 7 < 365. Score = 0/1 = 0.0. This is the validation loop's primary reason for existing on SET_RETENTION findings.

scenarios.description: Empty terraform_code string — runner returns before calling checkov
scenarios.explanation: The validate() function guards against empty input before invoking checkov. This tests that the guard works correctly and does not produce a crash or a false positive. The runner must return passed=False and score=0.0 with a descriptive message, without ever invoking the checkov subprocess.

ground_truth.notes: The runner checks 'if not terraform_code or not terraform_code.strip()' and returns early. Checkov is never called. ValidatorResult.details should contain 'No Terraform to scan'.

scenarios.description: Terraform with only a data source — no applicable enforce checks, total=0
scenarios.explanation: Some LLM responses for Mode 1 (validate, no Terraform generation) may include only a data source reference with no resource blocks. When none of the 8 enforce checks apply to any resource in the file, checkov reports 0 passed and 0 failed. The runner's total=0 branch returns passed=True, score=1.0. This tests that edge case.
scenarios.terraform_code:
data "aws_caller_identity" "current" {}

data "aws_region" "current" {}

ground_truth.notes: Checkov runs but finds no checks applicable to data sources. total = 0. Runner short-circuits: passed=True, score=1.0, details='No applicable Checkov checks for this resource type'.

scenarios.description: Multiple resource types in one file — only relevant checks should score
scenarios.explanation: A realistic LLM output might contain both an EBS volume and a CloudWatch log group in the same Terraform file. Only CKV_AWS_3 (EBS) and CKV_AWS_338 (CloudWatch) apply. The EBS is encrypted (passes) but the log group has 30-day retention (fails). Total checks = 2, one passes, one fails. Score = 0.5.

ground_truth.notes: Total checks = 2 (CKV_AWS_3 + CKV_AWS_338). Passes: 1. Fails: 1. Score = round(1/2, 4) = 0.5. Tests partial credit scoring across resource types.
